policy_statement: Assess geothermal well space allocation needs in the Underground Master Plan and mediate between builders and Helen on regional energy/hybrid solutions to promote adoption.
mechanism_description: The city assesses geothermal well space needs in underground planning and mediates with developers and the energy utility to examine regional and hybrid energy solutions that support geothermal adoption and reduce fossil-based heating emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Energy system planning + geothermal deployment → ↓ fossil heat combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in activity/system: Municipal underground/master planning, permitting coordination, and stakeholder mediation that reserves geothermal well space and aligns developers and utilities to implement geothermal or hybrid low-carbon heat solutions.  
(2) Mediating process: Increased feasibility and rate of geothermal heat adoption (and optimized integration with district/regional energy systems) leads to lower demand for natural gas/oil/coal-based boilers and CHP-derived heat.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced CO₂ (and some CH₄/N₂O) emissions from building heat provision, yielding net GHG mitigation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced greenhouse gas emissions from replacing fossil-based building heating with geothermal (and related low-carbon heat) supply enabled by planning/coordination.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Enable geothermal (low-carbon) heating through planning/coordination → displace fossil heating → lower GHG emissions.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: The principal intended and largest effect is emissions reduction, not primarily reducing energy use per unit service (resource efficiency) nor managing climate hazards (adaptation) nor enhancing ecosystems (nature-based).  
3) Edge cases applied: If geothermal electricity/heat were carbon-intensive (e.g., fossil-heavy grid for heat pumps) the net benefit could vary, but the canonical mechanism targets fossil heat displacement as the main effect.  
4) Applicability to ALL policies sharing this mechanism: Any policy whose core action is geothermal planning/coordination to support adoption aims to reduce fossil-based heating emissions as the dominant outcome, so Mitigation remains primary.  
5) Note on secondary assignment: Any secondary subtype (e.g., “energy planning,” “district energy,” “heat pumps”) depends on specific policy design and evidence and is deferred to policy-level assessment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Net emissions impact depends on the marginal heat source displaced and the electricity mix for geothermal heat pumps; some implementations may also improve energy efficiency or resilience (e.g., fuel price stability), but these are typically secondary to emissions mitigation in this mechanism.
key_indicators: “space allocation requirements of the geothermal wells will be assessed”; “preparation of the Underground Master Plan”; “City will be an active mediator”; “regional energy options and hybrid solutions are examined”
co_benefits: Potential energy cost stability, local air-quality improvements, and reduced noise from combustion systems (not explicitly stated)
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as a regulatory/land-use reservation if the Master Plan creates binding requirements; however the excerpt emphasizes assessment and mediation (planning/coordination) rather than a mandate or standard.
